content.thinking: The user wants a full simulation script they can read - like the ones I wrote for Ethan and Carly earlier, but more complete with all the steps (20 minutes worth) and including the new objections about "is grad school worth it" and "is Vlerick internationally recognized" plus their personal connection points. Let me write two complete simulations - one for Ethan and one for Carly - that cover the full 20 minutes.

content.text:
## SIMULATION 1 — ETHAN (Driver)

---

**YOU:** "Hey Ethan, great to meet you — is now still a good time?"

**ETHAN:** "Yeah go ahead."

**YOU:** "Perfect. I'm Can — I'm actually an international student at Vlerick right now, originally from Istanbul. I saw your LinkedIn before this call — VP of External Relations at Schey, Gartner internship lined up — impressive stuff. Quick question before we dive in: did you grow up in Vermont?"

**ETHAN:** "Yeah, born and raised."

**YOU:** "Love that — I always find the people who moved somewhere for opportunity are the ones most open to this conversation. You went Vermont to Ohio, I went Istanbul to Brussels — same energy. What I'd love to do is ask you a few questions about where you're headed, share a bit about Vlerick, and see if there's a fit. Does 10-15 minutes work?"

**ETHAN:** "Sure."

---

**YOU:** "So you've got Gartner lined up this summer — what made you still consider grad school on top of that?"

**ETHAN:** "I want to move into leadership faster. Sales is great but I want the full business picture — strategy, finance, the whole thing."

**YOU:** "Interesting. And when you think about a grad program — what's the single thing that would make it worth taking a year out?"

**ETHAN:** "Honestly ROI. I need to know it opens doors, not just costs me a year and looks good on paper."

**YOU:** "Makes sense. Are you looking at other programs right now?"

**ETHAN:** "Yeah — a couple in the UK, maybe one in Ireland."

**YOU:** "Got it. So if I'm hearing you right — you want something that accelerates you into leadership, has a real ROI, and you're comparing Vlerick against UK and Irish schools. Is that fair?"

**ETHAN:** "That's exactly it."

---

**YOU:** "Ok — so let me tell you where Vlerick sits. Triple accredited — AACSB, EQUIS, AMBA. Less than 1% of business schools globally hold all three. Same standard as London Business School, HEC-Paris, the schools you're probably looking at. Financial Times ranks us #1 in Belgium, #33 in Europe. And for marketing specifically — which I know is your background — we're ranked #23 in the world."

**ETHAN:** "Ok that's solid."

**YOU:** "But here's the ROI piece that actually matters. 95% of Vlerick graduates are employed within 3 months. Average starting salary €70,000. And three years out — alumni report a 76% salary increase. That's not a Belgian number — that's a global number across 23,000 alumni in 100 countries."

**ETHAN:** "That's impressive. But will US employers actually know Vlerick?"

**YOU:** "Fair — and honest question. The name Vlerick is newer in the US than Oxford. But the accreditation isn't — AACSB, EQUIS, AMBA is the same flag US employers already recognize from schools they trust. And here's what actually happens in interviews: you walk in with a year in Europe, a network across 40 nationalities, experience most US candidates literally cannot replicate. That's not a gap on your resume — that's a conversation starter every time. Every interviewer asks about it."

**ETHAN:** "What about the cost though — is it worth it over a UK school?"

**YOU:** "Compare it directly — Durham, Aston, Smurfit — Vlerick is significantly lower tuition for equivalent or better accreditation. And cost of living in Belgium is 30-40% cheaper than London or Dublin. So you're getting a stronger academic brand at lower total cost. It's not the cheaper option — it's the smarter investment."

**ETHAN:** "Ok — but is a grad degree even worth it right now? I've got a strong offer already."

**YOU:** "I asked myself the exact same question before I came here. You already have momentum, strong internships, a real offer. Grad school on top of that doesn't slow you down — it puts you 5 years ahead of peers who wait. You're 22. The ROI on a year abroad at 22 compounds for the next 20 years in a way it simply doesn't at 32. The people who wait usually wish they hadn't."

**ETHAN:** "That's a good point."

---

**YOU:** "Based on everything we've talked about — the leadership acceleration, the ROI, the accreditation — how does Vlerick sound so far?"

**ETHAN:** "Actually pretty good."

**YOU:** "Then here's what I'd suggest: start the application now. No GMAT required, the process is straightforward. It gets you in the queue for a Vlerick interview and we can look at what financial support is available. No commitment beyond that first step — just opens the door. Does that make sense?"

**ETHAN:** "Yeah I think so. I'd want to look into it more."

**YOU:** "Totally fair — is there one specific thing holding you back I can help clear up before we wrap up?"

**ETHAN:** "Not really, I think I just need to do more research."

**YOU:** "Perfect — I'll send you the key links after this call. Great speaking with you Ethan."

---
---

## SIMULATION 2 — CARLY (Expressive)

---

**YOU:** "Hey Carly! Great to meet you — is now still a good time?"

**CARLY:** "Yes, so excited for this!"

**YOU:** "Love the energy already. I'm Can — I'm actually a student at Vlerick right now, originally from Istanbul. I checked out your LinkedIn before this — journalism AND sales at UKG, plus VP of People — that's a really rare combination. How did you end up going from journalism into sales?"

**CARLY:** "Honestly it just happened — I joined Schey not really knowing what I wanted and fell in love with it. Then UKG gave me the chance to try sales and I loved that too."

**YOU:** "That's actually a great story — and very relevant to what I want to share with you. What I'd love to do is ask a few questions about your grad school thinking, tell you about Vlerick, and see if it clicks. Does 10-15 minutes work?"

**CARLY:** "Absolutely!"

---

**YOU:** "So what first made going abroad feel like a real option — not just a dream?"

**CARLY:** "I think seeing people around me do it. And honestly I want something different before I go fully corporate. Like one real adventure first."

**YOU:** "I love that. What excites you most about that — and what worries you?"

**CARLY:** "What excites me is the experience, new people, traveling Europe. What worries me is being so far from home. And honestly whether my journalism background even fits in a business school."

**YOU:** "That's the most honest answer I've heard — and those are exactly the right concerns to have. So you want the adventure, the people, the experience — but you need to know you won't be alone AND that your background is actually a strength there. Is that fair?"

**CARLY:** "Yes exactly — you got it!"

---

**YOU:** "Ok so let me paint the picture. Vlerick is triple accredited — top 1% of business schools globally, #1 in Belgium, #33 in Europe. But honestly the numbers aren't what makes it special for someone like you. Here's what does:"

**YOU:** "Every single student at Vlerick left somewhere comfortable to be there. 40+ nationalities, nobody is local — which means everyone builds community incredibly fast. I'm from Istanbul — I didn't know a single person in Belgium when I arrived. Within three weeks I was traveling to Amsterdam with people from Brazil, Germany, and South Korea. You won't feel like a foreigner. You feel like a co-founder of something."

**CARLY:** "Oh that's so nice, I love that."

**YOU:** "And Brussels — best food in Europe, incredible city, Paris is 1h20 by train, Amsterdam two hours, London two hours. Your weekends literally become a different country every week. You come back to the US with a story nobody around you will have."

**CARLY:** "Ok that sounds amazing. But what about my journalism background — I'm genuinely not a pure business student."

**YOU:** "That's actually your biggest advantage — and I'll tell you why. *(boomerang)* In every group project, every presentation, every client pitch at Vlerick — the person who can write, communicate, and tell a story wins the room. Most business students cannot do that. You can. Vlerick actively recruits from diverse backgrounds because they know homogeneous cohorts produce average results. Your journalism background doesn't make you a worse fit — it makes you the most interesting person in the cohort."

**CARLY:** "Wow ok I actually never thought about it that way."

**YOU:** "And here's the career reality: 95% of Vlerick grads employed within 3 months, average starting salary €70,000, and we're ranked #23 in the world for marketing — which sits right at the intersection of everything you've been building."

**CARLY:** "But is a grad degree even worth it? I have UKG and things are going well."

**YOU:** "I felt the exact same way before I came. Here's what I found — you're 22, you have momentum, strong experience. A year at Vlerick doesn't pause that momentum — it multiplies it. You go back with an international network, a European business perspective, and a story that makes every interview more interesting. The people who wait until 30 always say they wish they'd done it younger."

**CARLY:** "That's so true honestly."

---

**YOU:** "Based on everything — the community, the experience, your background being an asset — how does Vlerick sound?"

**CARLY:** "Really good actually! I'm genuinely interested."

**YOU:** "Then my suggestion is start the application now. No GMAT, straightforward process, gets you into the queue for a proper Vlerick interview, and we can explore financial support options together. Just the first step — no pressure beyond that. Does that work?"

**CARLY:** "Yes I think I want to do that!"

**YOU:** "Amazing — I'll send you everything you need after this. So great speaking with you Carly, genuinely."

---

## THE DIFFERENCE IN ONE LINE

**Ethan** — facts, ROI, direct, short answers, let him process

**Carly** — warmth, stories, let her talk, emotional payoff, energy matches hers

**Good luck — you've got this 🔥**

content.thinking: The user wants to know the role play evaluation rubric. I already read this from the files earlier. Let me recall it and present it cleanly.

content.text:
Yes, read it from the files. Here it is:

---

## ROLE PLAY EVALUATION RUBRIC
*(scored 1-5 on each)*

---

**APPROACH**
- Professional introduction
- Adequately builds rapport
- Gains prospect's attention
- Sets an agenda for the meeting

---

**NEEDS IDENTIFICATION**
- Had effective questions/strategy built
- Determined concerns/needs/wants
- Gains understanding of prospect goals/outcomes

---

**PRODUCT/SERVICE PRESENTATION**
- Presented to prospect concerns/needs
- Uses features & benefits OR outcome & process technique
- Used appropriate visual aids, examples, stories
- Effective follow-up questions to determine area addressed

---

**OVERCOMING OBJECTIONS**
- Identifies objections
- Clarifies the objection
- Resolves/answers in a compelling way
- Confirms objection is no longer a concern

---

**CLOSE**
- Makes a recommendation on next steps
- Asked some type of commitment from the buyer

---

**COMMUNICATION SKILLS**
- Effective verbal communication
- Salesperson enthusiasm and confidence
- Salesperson knowledge of product/service

---

## WHAT THIS MEANS FOR YOU

| Where points are lost | How to avoid it |
|---|---|
| Skipping agenda | Always say "does that sound ok?" |
| No summary statement | "So what I'm hearing is..." before pitching |
| Pitching before needs | Questions FIRST, always |
| Ignoring objections | Never skip Listen→Clarify→Cushion→Respond→Confirm |
| Weak close | Always end with a specific recommendation + commitment ask |
| Low energy | Match Carly's excitement, command Ethan's respect |

**Every section is scored. Hit every step even briefly — process matters more than perfection.**

**ETHAN:** "Yeah, I've got about 15 minutes."

**YOU:** "Perfect, that's all I need. I'm Can — I'm an international student at Vlerick right now, originally from Istanbul. I looked at your profile before this — the Schey VP role, Gartner lined up — you've clearly been intentional about your career. Quick connection before we start: you moved from Vermont to Ohio for school — I moved from Istanbul to Belgium. Both of us bet on ourselves geographically. What I'd love to do is ask a few questions, share what Vlerick looks like, and see if there's a fit. Does that work?"

**ETHAN:** "Sure, go ahead."

---

**YOU:** "With everything you've built — Schey, the internships, Gartner — where do you feel like you still have a gap?"

**ETHAN:** "Honestly the international piece. All my experience is US-based. I feel like that could limit me long term."

**YOU:** "Interesting. What would international experience actually look like for you — is it the network, the exposure, something else?"

**ETHAN:** "Both. Understanding how business works differently in other markets. And having contacts outside the US."

**YOU:** "And when you think about grad school — is the degree itself important or is it more the experience around it?"

**ETHAN:** "Mainly the experience. The degree needs to hold weight though."

**YOU:** "So what I'm hearing — you want real international exposure, a global network, AND a degree that carries credibility back in the US. Is that right?"

**ETHAN:** "Yeah exactly."

---

**YOU:** "That's the sweet spot Vlerick hits. Let me give you the credibility piece first — triple accredited, AACSB, EQUIS, AMBA, top 1% globally, same standard as London Business School and HEC-Paris. Financial Times ranks us #1 in Belgium, #33 in Europe, #23 in the world specifically for marketing. The degree holds weight."

**ETHAN:** "Ok."

**YOU:** "Now the experience piece — Brussels is the headquarters of the EU and NATO. Every multinational you'll ever want to work for has their European decision-makers within walking distance. Your cohort will be 40+ nationalities. I came from Istanbul knowing nobody — within a month I had a network across three continents. You go back to the US with something your peers at US programs literally cannot replicate."

**ETHAN:** "That actually sounds good. But honestly — why not just work for a couple years first and then do it?"

**YOU:** "I hear that a lot — and here's the honest answer. *(cushion)* The people who say 'a couple years' usually mean five. Life gets more complicated — salary, relationships, responsibilities. You're 22, you have momentum, zero obligations. The ROI on doing this now versus at 27 is completely different. And Gartner will still exist when you get back — except you'll walk in with international experience most of their senior people don't have."

**ETHAN:** "Fair point. What about cost — Belgium sounds cheap but the program itself?"

**YOU:** "Compare it directly to your UK options — Aston, Durham, Smurfit. Vlerick is lower tuition for equivalent or stronger accreditation. Cost of living in Belgium is 30-40% cheaper than London or Dublin. And the outcome data — 95% employed within three months, average starting salary €70,000, 76% salary increase three years out. Run those numbers against the cost and it pays back faster than most people expect."

**ETHAN:** "My parents are a bit skeptical about Belgium specifically — they've never heard of it."

**YOU:** "Completely normal — most Americans haven't. *(feel, felt, found)* My family felt the same when I said Belgium. What they found out is that Brussels is one of the most internationally connected cities in the world — safer than most major US cities, English spoken everywhere, incredible quality of life. The concern is usually about the unknown, not the reality. Once people see what Brussels actually is, the skepticism disappears fast."

**ETHAN:** "Ok. And job placement after — does Vlerick actually place people in the US?"

**YOU:** "23,000 alumni in over 100 countries — the US is well represented. And honestly for someone with your sales background going back to the US market, you're not competing for Belgian jobs. You're going back stronger with a global credential and an international network. Top 15 in Europe for career progression — that's not a local ranking."

---

**YOU:** "Based on everything — the international exposure you're looking for, the accreditation, the career outcome data — how does Vlerick sound?"

**ETHAN:** "Actually pretty compelling."

**YOU:** "Then my recommendation is straightforward — start the application now. No GMAT required, process is quick, gets you into the queue for a proper Vlerick interview and we can look at financial support options. No commitment beyond that first step. Does that make sense?"

**ETHAN:** "Yeah I think I'd want to look into it more seriously."

**YOU:** "Perfect. Is there one last thing I can clear up before we wrap?"

**ETHAN:** "No I think I'm good. Thanks Can."

**YOU:** "Great speaking with you Ethan — I'll send you the key info after this. Good luck with Gartner this summer."

---

## NEW OBJECTIONS COVERED THIS TIME

| Objection | Technique used |
|---|---|
| "I'll just work a couple years first" | Direct reframe — timing argument |
| "My parents are skeptical about Belgium" | Feel, Felt, Found |
| "Does Vlerick place people in the US?" | Data + reframe |

content.text:
## THE SKELETON

**1. INTRO**
"Hi I'm Can from Vlerick — good time?"

**2. RAPPORT**
One thing about THEM

**3. VALUE STATEMENT**
One line why you're calling

**4. AGENDA**
"Ask a few questions, share Vlerick, see if there's a fit — sound good?"

**5. QUESTIONS**
Broad → Narrow → Closed
*(shut up, let them talk)*

**6. SUMMARY**
"So what I'm hearing is X, Y, Z — fair?"

**7. VALUE PROPS**
Concern 1 → Concern 2 → Story

**8. TRIAL CLOSE**
"How does this sound so far?"

**9. RECOMMENDATION**
"Based on our conversation I'd suggest you apply now..."

**10. OBJECTIONS** *(anytime)*
Listen → Clarify → Cushion → Respond → Confirm

---

**Ethan** = ROI, direct, short

**Carly** = story, warm, let her talk

**Both** = Ghent is beautiful, 30min from Brussels, triple accredited, #1 Belgium, #23 marketing worldwide

**Go! 🔥**

content.text:
## QUESTIONS BANK

---

### FOR ETHAN (Driver — keep it short)

**Broad:**
- "What made you still consider grad school with Gartner already lined up?"
- "Where do you feel like you still have a gap after everything at Schey?"

**Narrow:**
- "What's the one thing that would make a year abroad worth it for you?"
- "Are you comparing Vlerick against other programs right now?"

**Closed:**
- "So ROI and career acceleration are the priorities — is that fair?"

---

### FOR CARLY (Expressive — let her talk)

**Broad:**
- "What made going abroad feel like a real option for you?"
- "What are you looking for that your current path isn't giving you?"

**Narrow:**
- "What excites you most — and what worries you?"
- "Is the journalism background something you're concerned about in a business school?"

**Closed:**
- "So the experience AND knowing your background fits — those are the two things that matter most?"

---

### WORKS FOR BOTH

- "What does your ideal grad school experience look like?"
- "What's holding you back from committing to something?"
- "What do the people closest to you think about going abroad?"

---

**Remember:** Ask ONE, stop talking, listen fully, then follow up. Don't rapid fire. 🔥

content.text:
That's actually the best lesson you could learn today — and you learned it live.

---

## What happened

You did what every expressive does under pressure — **filled the silence.** Nervous energy turns into talking. Talking kills the sale.

---

## Why it matters

His own slide said it:
- You talk **20%**
- They talk **80%**
- Ask one question → **shut up**
- Silence feels uncomfortable for YOU, not them

---

## How to fix it next time

**After every question — pause and count to 3 in your head before speaking.**

If they give a short answer — don't panic, just ask:
> "Tell me more about that."

or

> "What do you mean by that specifically?"

Those 6 words buy you time AND make them talk more.

---

## The real feedback

You knew the material. You knew the framework. You knew the value props.

The only thing that went wrong was **nerves → talking.**

That's not a knowledge problem. That's a reps problem. Do it 5 more times and it disappears.

---

**How did the rest go? Did you close?**

content.text:
Haha okay so she flipped the script on you — she became the salesperson and you became the buyer. Classic.

---

## What actually happened

She was playing **Analytical** not Expressive. Quiet, question-heavy, testing you. You expected talkative Carly, got a stone wall instead.

---

## What you should have done

**When she went quiet after your question:**
> "What's the first thing that comes to mind?"

**When she kept asking YOU questions:**
> "That's a great question — before I answer, help me understand what's driving that concern for you?"

Flip it back. Every time. You answer her question WITH a question.

---

## The one line that saves you

When someone keeps asking and you're losing control:
> "I want to make sure I answer what actually matters to you — what's the biggest thing on your mind about this decision?"

Resets the whole conversation. Gets them talking. You take control back.

---

## Real talk

- You knew the material ✅
- You knew the framework ✅
- You got an unexpected buyer style ✅
- You adapted on the fly ✅

First live roleplay under pressure with a professor watching — nobody nails it. The fact that you know exactly what went wrong means you'd destroy it the second time.

**What score did you get?**
